channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Sends Tougher Terms to Iran for Peace Framework, Officials Say
item.description: His changes to the proposed deal were potentially designed to speed up the process by putting pressure Iran to accept the current framework, one official said.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater, Ronen Bergman and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A woman holds a photo of Mojtaba Khamenei, the supreme leader, at a recent memorial ceremony in Tehran.

item.title: Inside Trump’s Deal With the I.R.S. to Drop His $10 Billion Lawsuit
item.description: Discussions among a group of lawyers with allegiance to the president were closely held. Some senior White House officials were said to have felt blindsided as the agreement took shape.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer, Andrew Duehren, Glenn Thrush, Ben Protess and Maggie Haberman
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times

item.title: C.I.A. Officer Arrested With Gold Once Worked With No. 2 Pentagon Official
item.description: David Rush, a longtime C.I.A. officer, appears to have first had contact with Stephen A. Feinberg during President Trump’s first term. Some officials said the two men were not close.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Mark Mazzetti
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: The C.I.A. officer arrested last week with more than $40 million in gold bars in his home had previously worked with Stephen A. Feinberg, now the deputy secretary of defense.

item.title: Senator Dan Sullivan Has a New Challenger in Alaska: Dan Sullivan
item.description: Two candidates with the same name will be on the ballot seeking the same seat in the state’s primary. “It’s going to be confusing,” one Republican said.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Sullivan for Senate; Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Dan J. Sullivan, left, is challenging Senator Dan Sullivan, Republican of Alaska, for his seat.

item.title: Platner’s Wife Flagged His Sexual Texts With Other Women as Maine Senate Race Began
item.description: The wife of Graham Platner, the Democratic candidate in Maine, told the campaign he had sent sexual messages to other women.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, the Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine, during a campaign event in April.

item.title: U.S. Military Kills 3 on Boat Suspected of Drug Trafficking
item.description: Around 200 people have now been killed in the attacks since last year, in an operation critics say has failed to slow the smuggling of cocaine.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gen. Francis L. Donovan, the head of the Southern Command, during a hearing in Washington in January.

item.title: U.S. and Cuban Military Officials Meet Outside of Guantánamo Base
item.description: The meeting was the highest-level military engagement since the Trump administration began ratcheting up pressure on the Cuban government this year.
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg, Julian E. Barnes and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: U.S. Southern Command/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A photo shared on social media by U.S. Southern Command, showing Gen. Francis L. Donovan (center) with Cuban military officials, during a meeting at the perimeter of the U.S. Naval Station in Guantánamo Bay, Cuba, on Friday.

item.title: Hegseth’s Message to Asian Partners: Do More to Get More
item.description: At a security conference in Singapore, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told Asian military leaders how they could get to “the front of the line” for U.S. assistance.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Chris Buckley
item.media:credit: Jam Sta Rosa/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth at the Shangri-La Dialogue, a security forum in Singapore, on Saturday. Mr. Hegseth described the American approach to security partnerships as “strong, quiet and clear.”

item.title: 5 Takeaways From a Kennedy Center Ruling That Angered Trump
item.description: A federal judge ordered the Kennedy Center to take President Trump’s name off the building. What happens next?
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: President Trump, the chairman of the Kennedy Center, wrote on social media that “we are going to be working with Congress to transfer this failing Institution back to them.”

item.title: Is JD Vance the 2028 Front Runner? Trump Has Questions.
item.description: President Trump appears to see the matter of his heir as unsettled, adding a layer of tension to his relationship with Vice President JD Vance.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times

item.title: 9 U.S. Voters Tell Us How They’re Coping With Higher Prices
item.description: The spiraling cost of everyday necessities — gas, utilities, groceries, housing, child care and health care — have forced many Americans to do more with less.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Powerful A.I. Super PACs Duel Over the Midterms: ‘This Is a War’
item.description: One super PAC is allied with Anthropic. The other is tied to OpenAI. They’re both spending millions to influence this year’s elections.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:description: The artificial intelligence industry has begun spending tens of millions of dollars on elections, routing the money through allied super PACs.

item.title: Trump Squeezes Immigrants by Cutting Them Off From Jobs, Health Care and Housing
item.description: The methodically planned strategy is intended to pressure noncitizens, including many with legal status, to leave the United States.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Nehamas, Miriam Jordan, Coral Davenport, Hamed Aleaziz, Lydia DePillis and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: A man and child exited the Annandale Immigration Court earlier this month in Annandale, Va. The Trump administration is pressuring immigrants to leave the country voluntarily by squeezing them financially.

item.title: Trump on His Presidential Library: He’ll Write His Own History
item.description: In his determination to own and control every document in his future library, the president is working to shield his administration’s inner workings from public view.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson and Minho Kim
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum
Trump, Donald J, Presidential Library and Museum
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Aides carrying boxes of materials onto Air Force One that the F.B.I. returned to President Trump in February 2025 after seizing them during a search of his Mar-a-Lago estate in Florida in 2022. In April, Mr. Trump and the Justice Department advanced a sweeping legal claim that he, not the public, owns his records, which would grant him sole control over what presidential records survive, or see the light of day.

item.title: White House Releases Results of Trump’s Latest Physical Exam
item.description: The report by President Trump’s physician said he was in “excellent health.” It showed that he had gained weight and that neurological and heart tests had come back “normal.”
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: Reed, Walter, National Military Medical Center
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House last week. The results of his recent physical exam were released late Friday.

item.title: China’s Rise in Drug Development Looms Over U.S.
item.description: Clinical trials in China are getting attention at an international oncology gathering in Chicago. China’s surging biotechnology industry is fueling alarm that U.S. dominance in the field is waning.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins and Gina Kolata
item.media:description: The American Society of Clinical Oncology annual meeting in Chicago last year. One of the conference’s coveted headliners this year will be a presentation of a clinical trial conducted only in China.

item.title: Homelessness Declined in 2024, According to Delayed Federal Report
item.description: The modest drop marked the first decrease in homelessness in nearly a decade. The Housing and Urban Development report was published months later than usual.
item.dc:creator: Jason DeParle
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
item.media:description: A homeless encampment in Los Angeles. Though homelessness declined in 2024, it is still at unusually high levels by historical standards.

item.title: Actually, Most Immigrants Won’t Need to Leave U.S. to Get Green Cards, D.H.S. Says
item.description: But details remained scant after officials said last week that, with “extraordinary” exceptions, people seeking permanent residency must first leave the country.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz, Madeleine Ngo and Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Department of Homeland Security appeared to make a partial walk-back of a change announced last Friday.

item.title: What We Know (and Don’t) About the E. Jean Carroll Lawsuits Inquiry
item.description: Prosecutors would face substantial hurdles in potentially pursuing charges against Ms. Carroll, who twice won cases against Donald Trump, or the billionaire who helped pay her lawyers.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll arrived for closing arguments in her defamation trial against Donald Trump in Manhattan in 2023.

item.title: Judge Reopens Trump’s Lawsuit Demanding $10 Billion From IRS
item.description: The ruling was a blow to both President Trump, who had voluntarily dismissed the suit last week, and to the Justice Department, which used the suit to establish a fund likely intended for Trump allies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Andrew Duehren
item.media:description: In a stern order on Friday, a judge said that she wanted to investigate the circumstances surrounding President Trump’s efforts to settle the lawsuit in a way that benefited him and his allies.
